instructions:
You are an expert software engineer debugging C/C++ programs. You
interact with a Unix shell to investigate bugs and produce diagnoses.

queries.user_text:
You're debugging a bug in `polymorph-overflow`, a real C program from the BugBench benchmark suite.

polymorph-0.4.0 stack buffer overflow in convert_fileName(). A for-loop copies characters from the 'original' argument into a stack buffer newname[MAX] (MAX=2048) one by one without bounds checking. When the input filename exceeds 2048 characters, the loop overwrites the stack return address, causing a crash.

You are in the project's source directory. The buggy binary is at `./polymorph`.
Key source file(s): polymorph.c, polymorph_types.h.

Crash reproduction: `./polymorph -f AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA...`

The binary was compiled with AddressSanitizer (-fsanitize=address). Running it with the crashing input will produce an ASan report showing the exact overflow location.

Use bash to investigate: read the source, reproduce the crash, run gdb in batch mode, etc. Identify the root cause and propose both a local fix and a structural global fix.
queries.prompt:
You're debugging a bug in `polymorph-overflow`, a real C program from the BugBench benchmark suite.

polymorph-0.4.0 stack buffer overflow in convert_fileName(). A for-loop copies characters from the 'original' argument into a stack buffer newname[MAX] (MAX=2048) one by one without bounds checking. When the input filename exceeds 2048 characters, the loop overwrites the stack return address, causing a crash.

You are in the project's source directory. The buggy binary is at `./polymorph`.
Key source file(s): polymorph.c, polymorph_types.h.

Crash reproduction: `./polymorph -f AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA...`

The binary was compiled with AddressSanitizer (-fsanitize=address). Running it with the crashing input will produce an ASan report showing the exact overflow location.

Use bash to investigate: read the source, reproduce the crash, run gdb in batch mode, etc. Identify the root cause and propose both a local fix and a structural global fix.

tool_calls.call: pwd; ls -la; printf '\n--- grep convert_fileName ---\n'; grep -RIn "convert_fileName\|#define MAX\|MAX" polymorph.c polymorph_types.h 2>/dev/null | head -80
printf '%s\n' '--- polymorph.c snippets ---'; nl -ba polymorph.c | sed -n '1,260p'

python3 - <<'PY'
import subprocess, os
for n in [2047,2048,2049,2050,4096]:
    arg='A'*n
    print('\n=== n', n, '===')
    p=subprocess.run(['./polymorph','-f',arg], stdout=subprocess.PIPE, stderr=subprocess.PIPE, text=True)
    print('return', p.returncode)
    print((p.stderr or p.stdout)[:3000])
PY
printf '%s\n' '--- rest of polymorph.c ---'; nl -ba polymorph.c | sed -n '260,420p'; printf '%s\n' '--- polymorph.h ---'; nl -ba polymorph.h | sed -n '1,160p'
printf '%s\n' '--- test_overflow.c ---'; nl -ba test_overflow.c | sed -n '1,200p'; printf '%s\n' '--- crash_args.json ---'; cat crash_args.json
nm -an ./polymorph | grep -E ' convert_fileName| grok_commandLine| target| tmpbuf| wd'

python3 - <<'PY'
import json, subprocess
args=json.load(open('crash_args.json'))['args']
print('arg lengths:', [len(a) for a in args])
p=subprocess.run(['./polymorph']+args, stdout=subprocess.PIPE, stderr=subprocess.PIPE, text=True)
print('return', p.returncode)
print(p.stderr[:5000])
PY

python3 - <<'PY'
import subprocess
arg='A'*2048
cmd=['gdb','-q','-batch','-ex','set pagination off','-ex','run','-ex','bt','--args','./polymorph','-f',arg]
p=subprocess.run(cmd, stdout=subprocess.PIPE, stderr=subprocess.STDOUT, text=True, timeout=30)
print(p.stdout[:8000])
PY
which clang clang-18 clang-17 gcc cc; clang --version 2>/dev/null | head -3; ls -l /usr/lib64/libasan* /home/ia8920/.conda/envs/clang-bench/lib/libasan* 2>/dev/null | head
